items.title: USDA lists April 2026 SNAP waiver rollouts: Texas (Apr. 1), Florida (Apr. 20), Colorado (Apr. 30)
items.summary: USDA Food and Nutrition Service’s SNAP Food Restriction Waivers summary lists three April 2026 implementation dates: Texas (04/01/26) restricting sweetened drinks and candy, Florida (04/20/26) restricting soda, energy drinks, candy, and prepared desserts, and Colorado (04/30/26) restricting soft drinks. The same USDA summary also lists Arkansas’s waiver beginning 07/01/26 restricting soda, certain drinks with less than 50% juice, other unhealthy drinks, and candy.

items.title: USDA press release details SNAP food-choice waivers for Arkansas, Idaho, and Utah starting in 2026
items.summary: USDA announced new SNAP waiver approvals for Arkansas (effective July 1, 2026), Idaho (effective Jan. 1, 2026), and Utah (effective Jan. 1, 2026) that expand items excluded from SNAP purchases beyond the nationwide exclusions. USDA says Arkansas will exclude soda (including low/no-calorie soda), certain fruit/vegetable drinks with less than 50% juice, other “unhealthy drinks,” and candy; Idaho will exclude soda and candy; and Utah will exclude soft drinks.

items.title: Medicaid/CHIP enrollment and renewals update: 76.8M enrolled in Oct. 2025; 71% of renewals retained coverage
items.summary: CMS’s Feb. 24, 2026 All-State Medicaid and CHIP call slides report 76.8 million people enrolled in Medicaid and CHIP in October 2025 (69.5M Medicaid and 7.2M CHIP), down 2.6 million (4%) from October 2024. The same slides report that among people due for renewal in October 2025, 71% had coverage renewed (49% ex parte), 19% were disenrolled (13% for procedural reasons), and 10% were pending.

items.title: SSA moves medical Continuing Disability Reviews (CDRs) to federal processing; cites backlog improvements
items.summary: SSA says it will transition medical Continuing Disability Reviews (CDRs) from State Disability Determination Services to SSA’s federal Disability Case Review site, aiming to free states to focus on initial disability claims. SSA says the DDS initial-claims backlog fell from over 1.26 million pending claims in June 2024 to 831,000 as of February 2026 (a reduction of more than 33%).

items.title: SSA OIG: 183 unimplemented audit recommendations could represent $4.9B in potential savings
items.summary: SSA’s Office of Inspector General says its annual oversight report (status as of Jan. 29, 2026) identified 183 unimplemented audit recommendations, representing about $4.9 billion in potential taxpayer savings and program improvements. SSA OIG says 94 recommendations remained open and 89 were previously reported as closed by SSA but were not implemented at the time of OIG’s review.

items.title: IRS: most EITC/ACTC refunds expected by March 2, 2026; filing deadline April 15
items.summary: IRS opened the 2026 filing season on Jan. 26, 2026 and reiterated that the deadline to file 2025 returns is April 15. IRS also says most refunds for the Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC) and the Additional Child Tax Credit (ACTC) are expected to be available by March 2, 2026 for taxpayers using direct deposit and with no other issues.

items.title: IRS EITC Awareness Day: Where’s My Refund projected dates by Feb. 21; most early EITC/ACTC refunds by March 2
items.summary: In its Jan. 23, 2026 EITC Awareness Day release, IRS says Where’s My Refund? will show projected deposit dates for most early EITC/ACTC filers by Feb. 21, 2026. IRS also reiterates that most early EITC or Additional Child Tax Credit refunds are expected to be available by March 2, 2026 for direct-deposit filers with no other issues.

items.title: HUD sets April 14, 2026 deadline for HUD-VASH additional administrative fee requests (supports voucher leasing)
items.summary: HUD’s revised PIH Notice 2026-01 announces $10 million in additional administrative fee funding for PHAs that administer HUD-VASH, with applications due April 14, 2026 (by midnight in the PHA’s time zone). HUD says the Full-Year Continuing Appropriations and Extensions Act, 2025 provided $15 million for HUD-VASH, including up to $10 million for administrative and other expenses, and the additional fees must be used only for HUD-VASH eligible expenses.
items.source: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development

items.title: HHS budget justification proposes eliminating LIHEAP funding in FY 2026 (from $4.025B to $0)
items.summary: HHS’s FY 2026 Administration for Children, Families, and Communities Congressional Justification states that the budget would eliminate funding for the Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP). The document’s tables show LIHEAP at $4.025 billion in FY 2024 and FY 2025, with a FY 2026 request of $0 (a change of -$4.025 billion).
items.source: U.S. Department of Health & Human Services

items.title: USDA WIC memo: FY 2026 CVV/B inflation adjustment 11.8% (vs FY 2022 baseline) and April 2026 deadline to add non-fresh produce options
items.summary: USDA FNS’s WIC Policy Memorandum #2026-2 states that FY 2026 cash-value voucher/benefit (CVV/B) amounts reflect an 11.8% inflation adjustment over the FY 2022 baseline. The memo also notes that state agencies must authorize at least one additional form of fruits and vegetables (frozen, canned, and/or dried) in addition to fresh by April 2026, and that as of November 2025, 95% of state agencies were already in compliance.

items.title: Federal Student Aid: maximum Pell Grant is $7,395 for 2026–27; some students can get up to 150% with year-round Pell
items.summary: Federal Student Aid says the maximum Federal Pell Grant award for the 2026–27 award year is $7,395. The same guidance notes some students may be able to receive up to 150% of their yearly Pell award if they attend an additional term in the same award year (often called “year-round Pell”), and it emphasizes that submitting the FAFSA is required to be considered.
